That 404 looks like it's coming from your own browser session testing against a different frontend/backend setup than the one I spun up in my sandbox (I have my own `.env.local` + a temporary backend running on port 8998 for verification). Two likely causes:

1. Your browser has a stale `api-port-override` value in `localStorage` from earlier testing, pointing at a URL that doesn't serve `/api/folders`.
2. `VITE_API_URL` isn't set in the environment your dev server is actually running in, so requests go to `undefined/api/folders` (relative to the frontend origin → 404).

Could you tell me: what URL are you running the frontend against, and is your backend actually up and reachable at that same URL? Or, as a quick check on your end — open devtools → Application → Local Storage and see if `api-port-override` is set to something stale, or check the Network tab for the exact failing request URL.

api.ts:27  POST http://localhost:5173/TERA_API_URL/api/folders net::ERR_ABORTED 404 (Not Found)

That confirms it — this isn't a bug from my refactor. It's a pre-existing quirk in `vite.config.ts`: it hardcodes

```js
const API_URL = "TERA_API_URL";
...
define: { "import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL": JSON.stringify(API_URL) }
```

So `import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL` is **always** the literal string `"TERA_API_URL"` when running plain `npm run dev`/`vite build` — it ignores any `.env`/`.env.local` file entirely. The placeholder only gets swapped for a real URL by `frontend/env.sh`, which runs as the Docker container's entrypoint (`docker-compose up`), not in local dev. This was true before my changes too — I didn't touch `vite.config.ts`.

Two ways to unblock your local testing:

1. **Use the app's own override** (already wired up, and it's the one I kept/restyled in Settings): open the app → **Settings → API Host Configuration**, set the URL to `http://localhost:8998`, click **Set**. That writes `api-port-override` to `localStorage`, which `api.ts` prefers over the placeholder. Reload after setting it.
2. **Run the real stack via Docker**: `docker-compose up` from the repo root, which runs `env.sh` to bake in `API_URL`/`APP_URL` from your `.env`.

Want me to fix the root cause instead (e.g., make `vite.config.ts` fall back to a real `.env` value for local dev when `TERA_API_URL` hasn't been replaced), or is the Settings-page workaround fine for now?
fix root cause

That second error (`react_devtools_backend_compact.js: Invalid argument not valid semver`) isn't from our code — it's the React DevTools browser extension failing to read a version string from the custom renderer that `@react-three/fiber` registers for the `<Canvas>` in `Viewer3D.tsx`. Custom React renderers like react-three-fiber don't expose a semver `version`, and the DevTools extension throws when it tries to compare it. This is a known, harmless cosmetic error from the extension itself (pre-existing, unrelated to this refactor, and not fixable from app code — it doesn't affect functionality). Safe to ignore.

Now let me verify the actual root-cause fix for the API URL issue.

`localhost:8998` is now correctly injected into `App.tsx`'s served module (confirming `import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL` resolves from `.env.local` instead of the literal `TERA_API_URL` placeholder). Let's confirm the actual API call succeeds end-to-end.
Root cause fixed and verified end-to-end (backend accepts the request; the frontend now correctly reads a real API URL instead of the unreplaced `TERA_API_URL` placeholder).
Let's take a quick visual screenshot to confirm the actual rendering matches the design intent.

Failed to launch 'bambu-connect://import-file?path=http://localhost:8998/api/models/09f1410b-13d6-4a5e-a095-824c4ee5d296/download' because the scheme does not have a registered handler.

That's expected browser behavior, not a bug — `Open in Slicer` links to a custom URI scheme (`bambu-connect://...`, from your selected default slicer in Settings), and the browser can only hand off to it if Bambu Studio is installed and has registered that protocol handler on your machine. If Bambu Studio isn't installed/registered, the browser will always show that "no registered handler" message — same as before my changes, this depends on your local OS/app setup, not the app's code. You can either install/register the slicer, or pick a different default slicer in Settings if you don't have one installed locally.

Let me continue confirming the visual refactor renders correctly.

[Request interrupted by user for tool use]

Failed to launch 'bambu-connect://import-file?path=http://localhost:8998/api/models/09f1410b-13d6-4a5e-a095-824c4ee5d296/download' because the scheme does not have a registered handler.

but i have bambu studio installed

Found the root cause — this isn't a bug in your URL string, it's a real limitation in Bambu Studio itself.

**What's happening:**
- `bambu-connect://` is not a "open this file in the slicer" handler at all — it's reserved for sending print jobs to a connected printer via Bambu Connect's cloud flow. It was never registered as a general URL scheme, which is exactly the "scheme does not have a registered handler" error you're hitting.
- The scheme that *is* meant for opening files, `bambustudio://open?file=<url>`, doesn't work on macOS at all, and even on Windows it has a domain allowlist that only trusts MakerWorld/Bambu-owned hosts — a self-hosted URL from your STLVault server gets silently rejected.

So there's no protocol string that will make "open in Bambu Studio" work reliably from a third-party server on macOS — this is a restriction Bambu built into the app, not something fixable from [api.ts](frontend/services/api.ts) or [Settings.tsx](frontend/components/Settings.tsx).

The only reliable workaround people use: download the `.3mf`/`.stl` file locally — macOS already associates those extensions with Bambu Studio by default, so double-clicking (or even the browser's own download-open) launches it.

How would you like to handle this in the app?

Now update the Settings.tsx description for Bambu Studio so it's not misleading about deep-linking, and check how the anchor tags in ModelList/DetailPanel use the href (whether they need a `download` attribute for it to trigger a save rather than navigate).

The backend already sends `Content-Disposition: attachment` on `/download` (via FastAPI's `FileResponse(..., filename=...)`), so falling back to that plain URL will correctly trigger a save dialog / download — same as clicking "Download" — rather than navigating the tab. Now updating the Settings description so it's not misleading.

Done. Summary of the fix:

- **Root cause**: `bambu-connect://` was never a file-open handler — it's Bambu Connect's print-job scheme. The actual file-open scheme (`bambustudio://open?file=`) is blocked on macOS entirely and restricted to Bambu-owned domains on Windows, so no protocol string would have worked for a self-hosted STLVault URL.
- **Fix**: [api.ts:119-135](frontend/services/api.ts#L119-L135) — when the user's slicer preference is Bambu, `getSlicerUrl` now returns the plain `/download` URL instead of the dead `bambu-connect://` link. The backend's `FileResponse(..., filename=...)` in [app.py:343](backend/app.py#L343) already sets `Content-Disposition: attachment`, so clicking "Open in Slicer" downloads the file, and macOS's default file association opens it in Bambu Studio.
- Updated the Bambu Studio description in [Settings.tsx](frontend/components/Settings.tsx#L26-L31) to explain this instead of implying a working deep link.

No behavior changed for OrcaSlicer/PrusaSlicer/Cura.

Base directory for this skill: /private/tmp/claude-501/bundled-skills/2.1.201/60f7df92df013f51961f43a270955245/run

**Running means launching the actual app and interacting with it** —
not the test suite, not an `import` of an internal function and a
`console.log`. The app as a user (human or programmatic) would meet
it: the CLI at its command, the server at its socket, the GUI at its
window.

## First: does a project skill already cover this?

A project skill that launches this app is the repo's verified path —
its author already cold-started from a Linux container and committed
what worked: the exact `apt-get` line, the env vars, the patches, the
driver. Use it instead of rediscovering.

```bash
d=$PWD; while :; do
  grep -Hm1 '^description:' "$d"/.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md 2>/dev/null
  [ -e "$d/.git" ] || [ "$d" = / ] && break
  d=$(dirname "$d")
done
```

- **One describes launching/driving this app** → read that SKILL.md
  and follow it verbatim. Don't paraphrase; don't skip the patches.
- **Mega-repo, several plausible, no clear match** → ask the user
  which unit to run.
- **Stale** (fails on mechanics unrelated to your task) → tell the
  user; offer to refresh it via `/run-skill-generator`.
- **Nothing about running** → fall back to the patterns below.

## Otherwise: match the shape, use the pattern

Pick the row closest to your project. Each example walks through
launch + first interaction; ignore any trailing "write the skill"
section — you're using the recipe, not authoring one.

| Project type | Handle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| CLI tool | direct invocation, exit code, stdin/stdout | [examples/cli.md](examples/cli.md) |
| Web server / API | background launch + `curl` smoke | [examples/server.md](examples/server.md) |
| TUI / interactive terminal | tmux `send-keys` / `capture-pane` | [examples/tui.md](examples/tui.md) |
| Electron / desktop GUI | Playwright `_electron` REPL under xvfb | [examples/electron.md](examples/electron.md) |
| Browser-driven | dev server + `chromium-cli` script | [examples/playwright.md](examples/playwright.md) |
| Library / SDK | import-and-call smoke script at the package boundary | [examples/library.md](examples/library.md) |

If nothing fits, start from the closest match and adapt. For a web
app, [examples/playwright.md](examples/playwright.md) — drive it with
`chromium-cli`, no custom driver needed. For a desktop app,
[examples/electron.md](examples/electron.md) — it has the `_electron`
REPL driver skeleton and the tmux wrapping.

## Drive it, don't just launch it

Launching with no interaction proves the entrypoint resolves. That's
not running the app — it's typechecking with extra steps. Drive it to
a point where a user would see something:

- CLI → type a representative command, check the exit code and output.
- Server → hit the route the diff touches with `curl`, read the body.
- TUI → `send-keys` a navigation, `capture-pane` the result.
- GUI → click the button, screenshot the window. **Look at the
  screenshot.** A blank frame is a failure to launch.

If the fallback pattern didn't work out of the box — you had to
install packages, set env vars, patch config, or write a driver —
recommend `/run-skill-generator` in your report so that work gets
captured as a project skill. If it just worked, don't.

## Summary

Added cookie-based session auth with a hardcoded admin account:

**Backend** ([app.py](backend/app.py)):
- `ADMIN_USERNAME`/`ADMIN_PASSWORD` env vars (default `admin`/`admin123`)
- `/api/auth/login`, `/api/auth/logout`, `/api/auth/me` endpoints; login issues an `HttpOnly`, `SameSite=Lax` session cookie backed by an in-memory token set
- All existing routes moved onto an `APIRouter` guarded by a `require_auth` dependency
- CORS tightened from wildcard to the exact `WEBUI_URL` origin (required for cookies to work cross-origin)
- Verified via curl: unauthenticated → 401, bad creds → 401, good login → cookie set, subsequent request → 200, logout → cookie invalidated → 401 again. Also confirmed CORS preflight/credential headers are correct for the real cross-origin dev setup.

**Frontend**: new [Login.tsx](frontend/components/Login.tsx), [App.tsx](frontend/App.tsx) gates the whole UI behind a session check (`api.getMe()`) and shows Login until authenticated; logout button in [Navbar.tsx](frontend/components/Navbar.tsx). All API calls now send `credentials: "include"` (via a new `authFetch` wrapper in [api.ts](frontend/services/api.ts) that also auto-redirects to Login on a 401), and the three.js loaders in [Viewer3D.tsx](frontend/components/Viewer3D.tsx) now set `withCredentials` so the 3D preview still works once file downloads are behind auth.

**Config**: added `ADMIN_USERNAME`/`ADMIN_PASSWORD` to `.env` and `docker-compose.yml`.

One thing to flag: I found your frontend dev server (port 5173) and Docker backend (port 8998) already running with your app open in a browser tab — I left both untouched, but since the backend image bakes in `app.py` at build time, **your running Docker backend won't have the new auth code until you rebuild it** (`docker compose up --build backend`). The live frontend already hot-reloaded my changes, so it's likely showing a login screen that can't authenticate yet until you rebuild. I did not run that rebuild myself since it affects your running containers — let me know if you'd like me to.
